nodes.context: Southeast Asian Islamist militant group based in Indonesia, dedicated to establishing an Islamic state in Southeast Asia.
Global Islamist militant network founded by Osama bin Laden.
Islamist separatist group in the Philippines.
Splinter cell of Jemaah Islamiyah formed by Abu Bakar Baasyir.
Radical Islamist/anti-colonialist movement in Indonesia in the 1940s.
Radical group in Indonesia that was cracked down by Suharto's New Order.
Islamist militant group in the Philippines.
Breakaway faction of the Moro National Liberation Front.
Organization created by Abu Bakar Bashir to connect Islamist groups in Indonesia.

nodes.standard_name: Al-Qaeda Jihad Organisation for the Malay Archipelago
nodes.original_text_name: Al-Qaeda Jihad Organisation for the Malay Archipelago
nodes.context: Splinter group from Jemaah Islamiyah formed by Noordin Top and Azahari Husin.
Tamil separatist group in Sri Lanka.
Southeast Asian Islamist militant group based in Indonesia, dedicated to establishing an Islamic state in Southeast Asia.

edges.evidence_quote: In addition to al-Qaeda, the group is also alleged to have links to the Moro Islamic Liberation Front and Jamaah Ansharut Tauhid
edges.context: JI is alleged to have links to al-Qaeda.

edges.evidence_quote: In addition to al-Qaeda, the group is also alleged to have links to the Moro Islamic Liberation Front and Jamaah Ansharut Tauhid
edges.context: JI is alleged to have links to MILF.

edges.evidence_quote: Jamaah Ansharut Tauhid, a splinter cell of the JI which was formed by Abu Bakar Baasyir on 27 July 2008.
edges.context: Jamaah Ansharut Tauhid is a splinter cell of JI.

edges.evidence_quote: JI has its roots in Darul Islam (DI, meaning "House of Islam"), a radical Islamist/anti-colonialist movement in Indonesia in the 1940s.
Bashir and Sungkar were both imprisoned by the New Order administration of Indonesian president Suharto as part of a crackdown on radical groups such as Komando Jihad
edges.context: JI leaders were imprisoned alongside Komando Jihad members, but no direct relationship between the groups is stated.

edges.evidence_quote: Recruiting, training, indoctrination, financial, and operational links between the JI and other militant groups, such as al-Qaeda, the Abu Sayyaf Group (ASG), the Misuari Renegade/Breakaway Group (MRG/MBG) and the Philippine Rajah Sulaiman movement (RSM) have existed for many years.

Recruiting, training, indoctrination, financial, and operational links between the JI and other militant groups, such as al-Qaeda, the Abu Sayyaf Group (ASG), the Misuari Renegade/Breakaway Group (MRG/MBG) and the Philippine Rajah Sulaiman movement (RSM) have existed for many years.

Recruiting, training, indoctrination, financial, and operational links between the JI and other militant groups, such as al-Qaeda, the Abu Sayyaf Group (ASG), the Misuari Renegade/Breakaway Group (MRG/MBG) and the Philippine Rajah Sulaiman movement (RSM) have existed for many years.
It is suspected by some to be linked to al-Qaeda or the Taliban.
edges.context: JI is suspected to be linked to the Taliban.

edges.evidence_quote: In 2004, Abu Bakar Bashir created the Indonesian Mujahedeen Council to connect Islamist groups, including JI, in Indonesia.
edges.context: JI is connected through the Indonesian Mujahedeen Council.

edges.target: Al-Qaeda Jihad Organisation for the Malay Archipelago
edges.evidence_quote: in 2006 Indonesian police reported that he and Azahari Husin had split from the organisation to form "an even more hardline group" which reportedly bore the name "Al-Qaeda Jihad Organisation for the Malay Archipelago".
edges.context: Al-Qaeda Jihad Organisation for the Malay Archipelago is a splinter group from JI.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Al-Qaeda, Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (until 2009)
edges.context: JI is allied with al-Qaeda according to infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Allies: Al-Qaeda, Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam (until 2009)
edges.context: JI was allied with LTTE until 2009 according to infobox.

edges.evidence_quote: Opponents: State opponents United Nations, Malaysia, Indonesia, Non-state opponents Moro National Liberation Front, Moro Islamic Liberation Front, Free Papua Movement, Free Aceh Movement (until 2005), Abu Sayyaf (until 2024)
edges.context: JI considers MNLF an opponent according to infobox.
